He **founded and edited several newspapers**, including **Vande Mataram and Arya Gazette**, which became platforms for disseminating nationalist ideas. **Rai also authored several books, including Young India, England’s Debt to India, The Story** **of My Deportation, Unhappy India,** and a series of popular biographies on Mazzini, Garibaldi, and Swami Dayanand. His literary contributions were vital in shaping public opinion against colonial rule. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Partition & Independence
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the 1946 General Elections in India:
I. 	 The elections were conducted in both the
British Indian provinces and the princely states.
II. 	 The Indian National Congress secured a
majority in Sindh province.
III. 	The All-India Muslim League emerged with a
majority in the North-West Frontier Province (NWFP).
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
After the failure of the **Simla conference**, Lord Wavell announced general and provincial elections, after which a Constitution-making body was to be set up. The last general elections in colonial India were held in 1945-46. These elections to the Central and Provincial legislatures were important because they formed the basis of a Constituent Assembly to decide India’s Constitution.
● **The elections were held only in the British Indian provinces, not in the princely states.** The princely states did not participate in the electoral process, as their representatives to the Constituent Assembly were nominated by the rulers, not elected. **So, statement I is not correct.** **Performance of the Indian National Congress (INC):**
● The INC got 91 per cent of non-Muslim votes. It captured 57 out of 102 seats in the Central Assembly.
● In the provincial elections, it got a majority in most provinces except in Bengal, Sindh and Punjab. The Congress majority provinces included the North-West Frontier Province (NWFP) and Assam, which were being claimed for Pakistan.
● Hence, **the INC gained a majority in Assam, but not in Sindh**. In Sindh, the Muslim League secured a majority and formed the government. **So, statement II is not correct.**
**Performance of the Muslim League:**
● The Muslim League got 86.6 per cent of the Muslim votes. It captured the 30 reserved seats in the Central Assembly.
● In the provincial elections, **it got a majority in Bengal and Sindh**. Unlike in 1937, now the League clearly established itself as the dominant party among Muslims.
● In Punjab, a Unionist-Congress-Akali coalition under Khizr Hayat Khan assumed
● Power.
● Hence, **the Muslim League gained a majority in Bengal, whereas, the INC gained** **a majority in NWFP. So, statement III is not correct.** **So, none of the above statements is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Political Party / Association
Leader
I. - Congress Socialist Party
Swami Sahajanand Saraswati
II. - Unionist Party Chhotu Ram
III. - Justice Party              C. Rajagopalachari
IV. - Hindu Mahasabha Madan Mohan Malviya
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Congress Socialist Party:** Congress Socialist Party was established in 1934 by the efforts of **Acharya** **Narayan Deo, J.P. Narayan, Ram Manohar Lohiya and Yusuf Meherally** within the Indian National Congress (INC) to develop a classless society with an equitable distribution of resources. **Swami** **Sahajanand Saraswati was not one of its leaders. So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Unionist Party:**  In 1923, the Unionist Party was formed in British Punjab by **Sir Chhotu Ram** of Rohtak district with the help of Sir Fazl-i-Husain. The party’s main agenda was to protect the agrarian society’s interests and curtail the growing communal sentiments in the region. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
○ Chhotu Ram believed that Swarjya would be easier to attain by entering the assemblies and councils and occupying more official power. He advocated for proper representation of the peasants in the assemblies, boards, and local bodies and emphasized Hindu-Muslim unity. The Unionist Party opposed the British government’s plan for the division of India. It fought for the rights of the masses and promoted unity amongst its citizens. Justice Party:
● **The Justice Party,** officially known as the South Indian Liberal Federation, was established in 1916 and emerged as a significant political force in the Madras Presidency. It primarily represented the interests of non-Brahmin communities. **Founded by Dr. T. M. Nair, P. Tyagaraja Chetty, and C. Natesa** **Mudaliar,** the party sought to challenge the prevailing Brahmin dominance in government jobs and education.
○ Periyar, also known as E.V. Ramasamy, was elected leader of the Justice Party in 1938. In 1944, the Justice Party merged with the Self-Respect Movement and was renamed as Dravidar Kazhagam. ○ **Chakravarti Rajagopalachari was a leader of the Indian National Congress, not the Justice** **Party. So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **Hindu Mahasabha:** Hindu Mahasabha, also known as Akhil Bhartiya Hindu Mahasabha, is a political organization founded in 1907 to protect Hindu interests and establish Hindu Rashtra based on the Hindu majority line. It expanded to the All-India Level in 1915. Hindu Sabhas emerged across India, including Punjab, United Province, Bihar, and Bombay presidency, but only after the 1909's Morley-Minto reform introduced separate Muslim electorates, did Hindu leaders unite and form an all-India organization.
○ It was **founded by Madan Mohan Malviya**. Other leaders like Lala Lajpat Rai and V.D Savarkar played significant roles in shaping the ideology of Hindu Rashtra.
○ Many policies and objectives of the organization varied from the secular approach of the Indian National Congress to the ideas of non-violence of Mahatma Gandhi. **So, pair IV is correctly** **matched.** **So, only two of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Partition & Independence
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Interim Government formed in 1946:
I. 	 It was formed to assist the transition of British
India to two independent nations.
II. 	 Jawaharlal Nehru held the post of the
President of the Interim Government.
III. 	It functioned under two Viceroys, Lord
Wavell and Lord Mountbatten.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Cabinet Mission of 1946** was a turning point in India’s struggle for independence. The mission aimed to devise a plan for the peaceful transfer of power while addressing the competing demands of the Indian National Congress (INC) and the Muslim League. The Cabinet Mission arrived in Delhi on March 24, 1946, and engaged in extended discussions with leaders of various political groups, focusing on two key issues: the formation of an interim government and the principles for drafting a new constitution to grant India independence.
● The interim government, which was formed on September 2, 1946, from the newly elected Constituent Assembly, had 69 per cent seats allocated to the INC. **The interim government was formed to assist in** **the transition of British India to the two independent nations of India and Pakistan. So, statement** **I is correct.**
● The Viceroy’s Executive Council, which was the governing council of British India, was replaced by the interim Cabinet. **Jawaharlal Nehru of the Congress was appointed as the vice president**, with the powers of the prime minister bestowed upon him. **The post of the President was held by the Governor** **General of India, who was still British. So, statement II is not correct.**
○ The 12-member Cabinet was formed by a coalition of Congress and Muslim League members. The Viceroy of India and the Commander-in-Chief were also a part of the Cabinet, apart from the twelve Indians. Other members of the government were from the All-India Muslim League, which had initially refused to join the cabinet and the then Commander-in-Chief, Sir Claude Auchinleck. While Muhammad Ali Jinnah was not a member of the cabinet, Liaquat Ali Khan who went on to become Pakistan’s first Prime Minister was part of the government. He was assigned the Finance portfolio and served as the first Finance Minister of India before independence. ○ The Interim Government, which remained in place until August 15, 1947, not only oversaw the partition but also drafted the post-independence Constitution of India and established diplomatic relations.
● The Interim Government functioned under two Viceroys: **Lord Wavell,** who served until 21 February 1947, and **Lord Mountbatten,** who succeeded him and held office from 21 February 1947 until India’s independence on 15 August 1947. S**o, statement III is correct.** **So, only two of the above statements are correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
**Ministers of Interim Government (September 2, 1946–August 15, 1947) and their** **portfolios:**
● Jawaharlal Nehru: Vice President of the Executive Council, External Affairs, and
● Commonwealth Relations
● Vallabhbhai Patel: Home, Information and Broadcasting
● Baldev Singh: Defence
● Dr. John Mathai: Industries and Supplies
● C. Rajagopalachari: Education
● C.H. Bhabha: Works, Mines and Power
● Rajendra Prasad: Agriculture and Food
● Jagjivan Ram: Labour
● Asaf Ali: Railway
● Liaquat Ali Khan (Muslim League): Finance
● Ibrahim Ismail Chundrigar (Muslim League): Commerce
● Abdur Rab Nishtar (Muslim League): Communications
● Ghazanfar Ali Khan (Muslim League): Health
● Jogendra Nath Mandal (Muslim League): Law

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Partition & Independence
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the British Prime Minister Clement Attlee’s Statement of February 20, 1947:
I. 	 It was declared that the British would leave
the Indian subcontinent no later than 15 August, 1947.
II. 	 The British powers and obligations toward
the princely states would be transferred to the interim governments of either dominion.
III. 	The proposals were unanimously accepted
by both the Indian National Congress and the Muslim League.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
On February 20, 1947, British Prime Minister Clement Attlee announced in the House of Commons that Britain would withdraw from the Indian subcontinent, formally declaring its intention to end colonial rule in India**.**
● **A deadline of June 30, 1948, was fixed for the transfer of power,** even if the Indian politicians had not agreed by that time on the constitution. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● It was stated that the British would relinquish power either to some form of central government or, in some areas, to the existing provincial governments, if the constituent assembly was not fully representative, i.e., if the Muslim majority provinces did not join. **British powers and obligations vis-à-** **vis the princely states would lapse with the transfer of power, but these would not be transferred to** **any successor government in British India. So, statement II is not correct.**
● It was also stated that Lord Mountbatten would replace Lord Wavell as the viceroy. The statement contained clear hints of partition and even Balkanization of the country into numerous states and was, in essence, a reversion of the Cripps Offer. **The provision of transfer of power to more than one centre** **was acceptable to Congress** because it meant that the existing assembly could go ahead and frame a constitution for the areas represented by it, and it offered a way out of the existing deadlock. However, **the Muslim League rejected the proposals** and launched a civil disobedience movement to overthrow the coalition government in Punjab, as it felt emboldened by the statement. **So, statement III is not** **correct.** **So, none of the above statements is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following promised an immediate
transfer of power along with Dominion Status to India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**The Mountbatten Plan,** announced on June 3, 1947, was a historic proposal that led to the partition of British India into two independent dominions—India and Pakistan. It was formulated by Viceroy Lord Louis Mountbatten to address the growing communal tensions and the urgent demand for independence. The plan aimed to provide a swift and peaceful transfer of power while minimizing unrest.
**The Mountbatten Plan culminated in the passage of the Indian Independence Act by the British Parliament** **on July 5, 1947.** This Act, based on the principles outlined in the Mountbatten Plan, received royal assent on July 18, 1947, and was implemented on August 15, 1947. It formalized the creation of two independent dominions— India and Pakistan—effective from the same date, marking the end of British colonial rule in the subcontinent. The plan outlined the framework for the partition and transfer of power in India.
● **Partition of India: The plan proposed the division of British India into two dominions,** India and Pakistan. However, Independence for Bengal was denied. The accession of  Hyderabad to Pakistan was also rejected.
● **Autonomy and Sovereignty: Both new dominions were to be granted full autonomy and sovereignty**, allowing them to draft their own constitutions.
● **Princely States:** The princely states were given the option to join either India or Pakistan, based on geographical contiguity and the wishes of their people.
● **Constituent Assemblies:** Separate constituent assemblies were to be established for India and Pakistan to frame their respective constitutions.
● **Boundary Commissions:** Boundary commissions were set up to demarcate the borders between India and Pakistan, particularly in the provinces of Punjab and Bengal.
● **Punjab and Bengal Legislative Assemblies:** These assemblies would vote in separate groups (Hindus and Muslims) on partition, with a simple majority determining if these provinces would be partitioned.
● **Sindh:** Sindh would decide its own course through a legislative decision.
● **Referendums:** Referendums would be held in the North-West Frontier Province (NWFP) and Sylhet district to determine their future.
● **Independence Date:** Independence was set for August 15, 1947. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● **Cripps Mission:** Cripps Mission, sent in March 1942, was a British attempt to gain Indian cooperation for British war efforts in World War II with the offer of ‘Dominion Status’ for India.
○ Based on a draft by the All-India Committee chaired by Attlee, the Cripps Mission proposed that India would be granted Dominion Status after World War II, with full autonomy in internal and external affairs while remaining associated with the British Commonwealth. ○ A fully Indian Constituent Assembly would be formed, with members elected by provincial legislatures and nominated by princely states, to draft the new constitution. Provinces unwilling to accept the constitution could opt out and frame their own. ○ Meanwhile, the Viceroy’s powers would remain intact during the war, and defence would stay under British control until its conclusion.
● **Wavell Plan:** The Wavell Plan, named after the Viceroy of India, Lord Wavell, was first introduced during the Shimla Conference in 1945.
○ It aimed to address India’s growing demand for self-governance by restructuring the Viceroy’s Executive Council, providing equal representation for both Hindus and Muslims.
○ The plan sought to resolve the political deadlock by offering separate communal representations in governance. ○ The Shimla Conference was convened specifically to secure an agreement on the Wavell Plan. However, it failed as the Indian National Congress and the Muslim League could not reach a consensus. ○ Disagreements, particularly over the League’s demand to be the sole representative of Muslims, prevented the plan’s implementation and led to the collapse of negotiations. **Cabinet Mission:** The Cabinet Mission of 1946 was a turning point in India’s struggle for independence. The mission aimed to devise a plan for the peaceful transfer of power while addressing the competing demands of the Indian National Congress and the Muslim League. Headed by Lord Pethick-Lawrence, Stafford  Cripps and A.V. Alexander, it sought to maintain the unity of India, while offering  a constitutional framework for self-governance. The mission proposed the creation of a Constituent Assembly, a federal union with autonomy for provinces, and a complex grouping of provinces. Despite its efforts, the mission failed to reconcile the conflicting visions of the Congress and the Muslim League, setting the stage for India’s eventual partition and independence in 1947.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Indian National Congress voluntarily declared that it would help the British war effort during World War II.
**Statement II:** The Indian National Congress was in full sympathy with the victims of Fascist aggression.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is not correct but Statement II is
incorrect

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● When the **Second World War** began in September 1939 with Germany's invasion of Poland, the **British** **government**, under **Viceroy Lord Linlithgow**, unilaterally declared **India’s involvement in the Second** **World War** without consulting any Indian political leaders or representatives, which led to widespread discontent and resentment among the Indian public and leadership.
● **Gandhi, in a letter to the Viceroy, explaining the aims of the Individual Satyagrah** - “The Congress is as much opposed to victory for Nazism as any British citizen can be. But their objective cannot be carried to the extent of their participation in the war. **And since you and the Secretary of State for India** **have declared that the whole of India is voluntarily helping the war effort, it becomes necessary to** **make clear that the vast majority of the people of India are not interested in it**. They make no distinction between Nazism and the double autocracy that rules India”. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● While the Congress sympathized with the **victims of Fascist aggression and supported the fight against** **Nazism,** its leaders argued that a nation under colonial rule could not genuinely help others achieve freedom. **So, Statement-II is correct.**
○ Congress demanded that India be declared free or at least given effective self-government before offering support for the war. When the British refused, Congress ministries resigned in protest.
● In October 1940, **Gandhi launched a limited satyagraha** by select individuals, avoiding a mass movement that might disrupt Britain’s war efforts. This was to show that most Indians did not support the war while India remained unfree, making it clear that there was little difference between Nazism and British autocracy for Indians. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Constitutional Developments
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Regulating Act of 1773:
I. 	 It provided for the establishment of a Supreme
Court at Calcutta, with original and appellate jurisdictions.
II. 	 It exempted the Governor-General and the
Executive Council from the Supreme Court’s jurisdiction over their official acts.
III. 	It made the governors of Bombay and Madras
presidencies subordinate to the GovernorGeneral of Bengal.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**The Regulating Act of 1773** brought about the British government’s involvement in Indian affairs in the effort to control and regulate the functioning of the East India Company. It recognised that the Company’s role in India extended beyond mere trade to administrative and political fields, and introduced the element of centralised administration. This act was of great constitutional importance as:
● It was the first step taken by the British Government to control and regulate the affairs of the East India Company in India.
● It recognised, for the first time, the political and administrative functions of the Company, and it laid the foundations of central administration in India.
● The Act designated the Governor of Bengal as the ‘Governor General of Bengal’ and created an Executive Council of four members to assist him. The first such Governor-General was Lord Warren Hastings.
● **The Act made the governors of Bombay and Madras presidencies subordinate to the governor-** **general of Bengal,** unlike earlier, when the three presidencies were independent of one another. **So,** **statement III is correct.**
● The Act prohibited the servants of the Company from engaging in any private trade or accepting presents or bribes from the ‘natives’.
● The Act provided for the establishment of a **Supreme Court at Calcutta (1774)** comprising one chief justice and three other judges. **The court had original and appellate jurisdictions where all subjects** **could seek redressal. So, statement I is correct.**
● The directors of the Company were required to submit all correspondence regarding revenue affairs and civil and military administration to the government. Thus, for the first time, the British cabinet was given the right to exercise control over Indian affairs.
● The 1773 Act did not grant complete immunity to government servants.
● The **Amending Act of 1781 exempted the Governor-General and the Council from the Supreme** **Court’s jurisdiction over their official acts.** Similarly, it exempted the company’s servants from Supreme Court jurisdiction for official actions. This means, for non-official actions they were still within the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● It also excluded revenue matters and revenue collection issues from the Supreme Court’s jurisdiction. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer**.
**Knowledge Box:**
**Amending Act of 1781** The British Parliament passed the Amending Act of 1781, also known as the Act of Settlement, to correct the flaws in the Regulating Act of 1773.
● **Immunity for Governor-General and Council:** It exempted the Governor-General and the Council from the Supreme Court’s jurisdiction over their official acts. Similarly, it exempted the company’s servants from Supreme Court jurisdiction for official actions.
● **Revenue Jurisdiction Exclusion:** It excluded revenue matters and revenue collection issues from the Supreme Court’s jurisdiction.
● **Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court:** It stated that the Supreme Court would have jurisdiction over all inhabitants of Calcutta. It also required the court to apply the defendants’ personal law, which meant that Hindus would be tried under Hindu law and Muslims under Mohammedan law.
● **Appeals from Provincial Courts:** It stated that appeals from Provincial Courts could be directed to the Governor-General-in-Council rather than the Supreme Court.
● **Elevated Powers of Governor-General:** The Governor-General-in-Council was granted the authority to establish regulations for the Provincial Courts and Councils.
● However, the act was not a long-term solution to the company’s affairs and was followed by the Pitt’s India Act in 1784, which was a more radical reform.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Who among the following was chosen as the
first Satyagrahi in the Individual Satyagraha movement?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In response to increasing repression and the British refusal to grant constitutional concessions without Congress-Muslim League agreement, Gandhi launched the Individual Satyagraha in 1940.
● This limited movement aimed to show that Indian patience was not weakness, express disapproval of the war, and offer the British a peaceful option. Satyagrahis voiced anti-war sentiments, demanding freedom of speech. If not arrested, they would march toward Delhi, starting the ‘Delhi Chalo’ campaign.
● **Vinoba Bhave was chosen as the first satyagrahi, followed by Jawaharlal Nehru.** By May 1941, 25,000 people had been arrested. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Constitutional Developments
questions.questionText:
The Charter Act of 1793 separated the revenue
administration from the judicial functions in India.
Which of the following were the implications of this development?
I. 	 The District Diwani Adalat was designated as
the District Court and placed under a district judge.
II. 	 The collector was made responsible only
for the revenue administration with no magisterial functions.
III. 	It led to the disappearance of the Maal
Adalats.
Which of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Charter Act of 1793 extended the trade monopoly of the Company in India for another period of twenty years. Lord Cornwallis was the Governor-General of Bengal during the passage of the Act.
● The revenue administration was separated from the judiciary functions, and this led to the **disappearance** **of the Maal Adalat**s. S**o, statement III is correct.**
● **The District Diwani Adalat was now designated as the District, City or the Zila Court and placed** **under a district judge.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● **The collector was now responsible only for the revenue administration with no magisterial** **functions**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **The 1793 Act provided that the Home Government members were to be paid out of  Indian revenues,**
**which continued up to 1919.** The Montague-Chelmsford Reforms of 1919 and subsequently the Government of India Act, 1919, provided that the salaries of the Secretary of State for India and his assistants were to be paid out of the British revenues. So far, they were paid out of the Indian revenues. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
**Other features of the Charter Act of 1793:**
● The royal approval was mandated for the appointment of the Governor-General, Governors, Commander-in-Chief.
● The Act extended the overriding power given to Lord Cornwallis over his council to all future Governor-Generals and Governors of Presidencies. It gave the Governor-General more powers and control over the governments of the subordinate Presidencies of Bombay and Madras.
● Senior officials of the Company were debarred from leaving India without permission—doing so was treated as resignation.
● The Company, after paying the necessary expenses, interest, dividends, salaries, etc., from the Indian revenues, was to pay 5 lakh pounds annually to the British government.
● The Company was empowered to give licences to individuals as well as employees to trade in India. The licences, known as ‘privilege’ or ‘country trade’, paved the way for shipments of opium to China.
● The Act provided that the Commander-in-Chief was not to be a member of the GovernorGeneral’s council, unless he was so appointed.
● The Act established covenanted civil services, which led to a more structured administration and pensions for employees.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
With reference to the colonial history of India,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 By the 1790s, Lord Cornwallis organised
a regular police force headed at the district
level by the local zamindars.
II. 	 William Bentinck abolished the post of
Superintendent of Police, and the District
Magistrate was made head of the police force
in his jurisdiction.
III. 	The Indian Police Act, 1861 established
a system of civil constabulary for each
province.
IV. 	 The Fraser Police Commission of 1902
provided for the appointment of educated
Indians to positions equivalent to the
European officers.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**: In pre-colonial India, the governments, under the Mughals and other native states, were autocratic in nature and lacked a separate or formal police system.
● **Lord Cornwallis, in his Code of 1793,** set the rule of separating revenue collection from the administration of civil justice as a safeguard for property rights against abuse of power by revenue officials and their agents. **Lord Cornwallis, in 1793, decided to divest the zamindars of their policing duties** and instead **divided the districts into thanas or units of police jurisdiction** of twenty to thirty square miles, each placed under a new officer called daroga, who was to be appointed and supervised by the magistrates. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Cornwallis organised a regular police force to maintain law and order** by going back to and modernising the old Indian system of thanas (circles) in a district under a daroga (an Indian) and **a** **Superintendent of Police (SP) at the head of a district**. **Bentinck (governor-general, 1828-35)** **abolished the office of the SP.** The collector/magistrate was now to head the police force in his jurisdiction and the commissioner in each division was to act as the SP. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Police Commission appointed in 1860** **provided** for a **basic structure of a police establishment** **for the Indian empire** **that was enacted in the Police Act of 1861**. It **established** the system of a **civil** **constabulary** (a civilian police force) **for each province, headed by an Inspector-General of Police** at the provincial level and District Superintendents of Police at the district level, with Indians filling the lower ranks. **So, statement III is correct.**
● **The Police Commission of 1902 (also known as the Fraser Commission)**, appointed by Lord Curzon, aimed to make the police force more efficient and suppressive. While it recommended some improvements in the pay and conditions of Indian constables and head constables and opened some limited avenues for educated Indians in subordinate officer ranks, **it did not provide for their appointment to positions** **equivalent to European officers (who held the gazetted ranks).** **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Constitutional Developments
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following legislations provided
that the territories of India henceforth were to be
governed in the name of the British Crown?

options.d: The Government of India Act of 1858

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**: The Charter Act of 1833, also known as the **Saint Helena Act** passed by the British Parliament, **restructured** **the East India Company's governance in India**. It was passed because there was a fresh and more widespread agitation in Britain for the abolition of the Company and a direct takeover of the Indian administration by the government. The Company's monopoly of the tea trade with China was now abolished, and henceforth it was meant only to have political functions, and here too the Indian possessions of the Company were to be held in trust for the British Crown.
● **Though the Charter Act of 1833** extended the lease to 20 years of the Company. **Territories of** **India** **were to be governed in the name of the Crown.** This was due to growing agitation in Britain for the abolition of the Company and a direct takeover of the Indian administration by the government. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 It prohibited the employment of children
under 12 years of age.
II. 	 It fixed the maximum working hours for
children at 7 hours per day.
III. 	It fixed maximum working hours for both
women and men.
IV. 	 It provided for a weekly holiday for all
workers.
Which of the above are correct regarding the
Indian Factory Act, 1891?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
In nineteenth-century India, **working conditions in factories and plantations were extremely harsh**, with long hours and low wages for men, women, and children alike. The workplaces were overcrowded, poorly ventilated, and lacked basic safety measures. The first demand for regulating labour conditions came not from Indian reformers, but from the Lancashire textile lobby in Britain, who feared competition from Indian textiles produced with cheap and unregulated labour. This led to the appointment of a commission in 1875, though the first Factory Act was not passed until 1881.
● The **Indian Factory Act, 1881,** focused mainly on **child labour**, **prohibiting employment of children** **under 7,** limiting working hours for those aged 7–12 to nine hours per day, granting four holidays a month, and requiring hazardous machinery to be fenced off.
● The **Indian Factory Act, 1891,** raised the minimum working age for children from 7 to 9 and the maximum from 12 to 14**.**
○ **Increased the minimum age (from 7 to 9 years**). **So, statement I is not correct.** ○ **Reduced maximum working hours for children to 7 hours a day. So, statement II is correct.** ○ Fixed maximum **working hours for women at 11 hours per day with a one-and-a-half-hour** **interval (working hours for men were left unregulated). So, statement III is not correct.** ○ **Provided a weekly holiday for all. So, statement IV is correct.**
● However, **these laws excluded British-owned tea and coffee plantations**, where workers faced even worse exploitation. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Constitutional Developments
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement-I:**
The Indian Councils Act of 1892 provided for
marginal expansion of the legislative councils.
**Statement-II:**
The Indian National Congress demanded the
expansion of the legislative councils.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both statement-I and statement-II are correct
and statement-II explains statement-I.
options.b:
Both statement-I and statement-II are correct,
but statement-II does **not** explain statement-I.
options.c:
Statement-I is correct, but statement-II is
incorrect.
options.d:
Statement-I is incorrect, but statement-II is
correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Lord Cross's Act or the Indian Councils Act of 1892** was a response to the increasing political awareness and nationalist sentiment in India, particularly the formation of the Indian National Congress in 1885. Indians, especially those educated and politically active, were demanding greater representation and a voice in the legislative process.
● The Indian Councils Act of 1892 provided for marginal expansion of the legislative councils both at the centre and the provinces. **The number of non-official members was increased both in the central** **(Imperial) and provincial legislative councils.** These councils were to be constituted through selection rather than election: the local bodies would send their nominees, from whom the viceroy at the centre and the governors at the provinces would select the members of the legislative councils. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● In 1885, the Indian National Congress was founded. One of their earliest and consistent demands was the reform and **expansion of the legislative councils, both at the central and provincial levels**. Prior to the reforms, Indian representation in legislative councils was minimal.
o The Congress saw reform of the councils as the **“root of all other reforms”.** It was in response to
the Congress demand that the legislative councils be expanded that the number of non-official members was increased both in the central (Imperial) and provincial legislative councils by the Indian Councils Act, 1892. o The Congress saw increased Indian participation in these councils as essential to voice the
grievances of the Indian people directly to the government. Thus, the expansion of Councils by the **Indian Councils Act of 1892 provided** primary forums to voice the grievances of the Indian people directly to the government. **So, statement II is correct.** **So,** both statement-I and statement-II are correct and statement-II explains statement-I. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Constitutional Developments
questions.questionText:
Which of the following were the recommendations
of the Indian Statutory Commission of 1927?
I. 	 Abolition of dyarchy at the provincial level
II. 	 Separation of Burma from India
III. 	Need for safeguards to protect minority
communities
Select the correct answer from the codes given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The 1919 Act had provided that a Royal Commission would be appointed ten years after the Act to report on its working. In November 1927, two years before schedule, the British government announced the appointment of such a commission—the Indian Statutory Commission. The commission submitted its report in 1930.
● The **Indian Statutory Commission**, also known as the Simon Commission, constituted under the **Chairmanship of Sir John Allsebrook Simon** by a royal warrant in November 1927, consisted of seven other English members in whose knowledge and ability the British Parliament placed great trust. The appointment of an all-White commission offered a great stimulus to the Congress Party to act together with all other parties on an all-India political basis.  It arrived in India in February 1928.
● Recommendations of the Simon Commission 1927
● Its key recommendations included the **abolition of dyarchy at the provincial level** and introducing a system of responsible government in provinces, where ministers would be accountable to the legislature. **So, point I is correct.**
● It suggested the **separation of Burma from India** and emphasised the **need for safeguards to** **protect minority communities. So, points II and III are correct.**
● A federation of British India and the Princely States be established, and that communal electorates be continued. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Round Table Conferences
(RTC), consider the following information:
Round Table Conference
Issue discussed
Outcome
I. - First Structure of the Federal government
Deemed a failure
II. - Second Immediate establishment of a responsible government
Creation of the North West Frontier Province and Sindh
III. Third Complete independence
White Paper on the new Constitution for India In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Simon Commission submitted its report in 1930. **Three Round Table Conferences were called by the** **British government to consider the proposals**.
● First **Round Table Conference** ○ **Issues Discussed:** **The federal government structure;** The executive's accountability to the legislature; Separate electorates for "untouchables" as proposed by Dr B R Ambedkar; Tej Bahadur Sapru proposed an All-India Federation. The Muslim League agreed with this.
○ **Outcome:** It was generally agreed that if India was to develop into a federation, there were to be safeguards regarding defence and finance, while other departments were to be transferred. The Indian National Congress leaders continued the Civil Disobedience Movement during the First Round Table Conference and did not participate in the conference. As a result, the **First Round** **Table Conference was deemed a failure. So, row I is correctly matched.**
● Second **Round Table Conferences** ○ **Issues Discussed: Gandhi demanded the immediate establishment of a responsible** **government at the national and provincial levels**. He also stated that Congress was India's sole political representative; The idea of a separate electorate for untouchables be rejected, with the claim that they were Hindus and thus should not be treated as a minority; It was unnecessary to have separate electorates or special safeguards for Muslims or other minorities. ○ **Outcomes:** This session only widened the difference between Congress and minorities.; **MacDonald ended the session with an address announcing the Creation of two new Muslim-** **majority provinces, North West Frontier Province and Sindh;** the Formation of an Indian Consultative Committee; the setting up of a committee on franchise, finance and states, and held out the degrading and perilous possibility of a unilateral British Communal Award. **So, row II is** **correctly matched.**
● **Third Round Table Conference** ○ **Issues Discussed:** **In the Conference, the reports of the Sub-Committees appointed during** **the Second Round Table Conference were heard and formed the basis of discussions.** Some more details about the new constitution were settled. The Indian delegates tried to push through some progressive provisions, which were instantly put into cold storage. Similarly, the question of including a Bill of Rights for the citizens was shelved on flimsy excuses. ○ **Outcomes:** **In March 1933, the British Government came out with the White Paper** **containing the proposals, indicating the line on which the new Constitution of India was to** **take shape.** On this basis, the Government of India Act of 1935 was enacted. **So, row III is not** **correctly matched.** **So, in only two of the three rows is the information correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
With reference to the colonial history of Indian,
the Aitchison Committee and the Lee Commission were related to:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**: The **Civil Service system introduced in India by the East India Company** for the benefit of its commercial affairs was transformed into a well-structured machinery to look after the administrative affairs of the acquired territories in India. In fact, in the beginning, the term ‘civil service’ was used to distinguish the servants of the Company engaged in commercial affairs from those people employed in the military and naval services. **In 1878-79, Lytton introduced the Statutory Civil Service** consisting of one-sixth of covenant posts to be filled by Indians of high families through nominations by local governments, subject to approval by the Secretary of State and the Viceroy. **But Charles Wood, the president of the Board of Control, opposed it** on the ground that there was no institution in India, that could train the boys for the examination.
● **The Public Service Commission, appointed under Charles Aitchison (1886)**. It **recommended** the abolition of the distinction between "covenanted" and "uncovenanted" civil services and proposed a threetiered classification: Imperial, Provincial, and Subordinate Civil Services.
● **Lee Commission (1923), also known as the Royal Commission on the Superior Civil Services in** **India,** was appointed under the chairmanship of Lord Lee of Fareham. Its purpose was to examine the racial composition of the higher public services in India and to make recommendations regarding their Indianization. **It recommended that 40% of future entrants should be British, 40% Indians directly** **recruited, and 20% Indians promoted from the provincial service.** It also recommended the **establishment of a Public Service Commission** (which later became the Federal Public Service Commission under the 1935 Act). **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
With respect to the Imperial Civil Services during
colonial India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 In 1893, the House of Commons in England
passed a resolution supporting the holding of a simultaneous examination in India and England.
II. 	 The Government of India Act, 1919,
recommended the establishment of the Federal and Provincial Public Service Commission.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**: Indian nationalists, particularly the Indian National Congress, consistently demanded that the Indian Civil Service (ICS) examinations be held simultaneously in India and England to provide equal opportunities for Indian candidates, who otherwise faced significant hurdles in traveling to London. In 1893, the House of Commons, under pressure, passed a resolution supporting this demand.
● The Public Service Commission, appointed under Charles Aitchison (1886), recommended the raising of the maximum age, but not simultaneous examination. **In 1892-93, under the initiative of William** **Gladstone, the House of Commons passed a resolution for simultaneous examination**, though the Secretary of State was still opposed to it. But at the same time, the maximum age for examination was further lowered to the disadvantage of the Indians. **So, statement I is correct.**
● In response to the nationalist demands, the **Government of India Act of 1919 finally provided for a** **separate, not simultaneous, recruitment examination to the ICS** to be **held in India**; and under its provision, the first examination was held in Allahabad in February 1922. But the explicit provision for the establishment of a **Federal Public Service Commission** for the proposed Federation and a **Provincial** **Public Service Commission** for each province was mandated under the **Government of India Act of** **1935**, **not the 1919 Act. So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Constitutional Developments
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Charter Act of 1813 ended the East India Company’s monopoly over trade in India.
**Statement II:** In the early 19th century, the British government was under pressure from merchants and manufacturers to open Indian markets to British
products. **Statement III:** Free markets and individual economic freedom are the most efficient and beneficial ways to organize society.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement-II and Statement-III are
correct and both of them explain Statement-I
options.b:
Both Statement-II and Statement-III are
correct, but only of them explains Statement-I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements-II and III is
correct and that explains Statement-I
options.d:
Neither Statement-II nor Statement-III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
In England, the business interests were pressing for an end to the Company’s monopoly over trade in India because of a **spirit of laissez-faire** and the continental system by Napoleon, by which the European ports were closed for Britain. The Charter Act of 1813 sought to redress these grievances. Continental System is the name of the economic warfare that Napoleon started against England in 1806.
● **The Charter Act of 1813**, also referred to as the East India Company Act 1813, was a legislative measure passed by the British Parliament that extended the East India Company’s charter for an additional 20 years**.** Lord Minto I was the Governor-General of Bengal when the Act was passed. **The Act abolished the** **trade monopoly of the company in India** **i.e., the Indian trade was thrown open to all British** **merchants.** However, it continued the monopoly of the company over trade in tea and trade with China. The Charter Act of 1833 marked the complete abolition of the Company’s commercial activities, ending all its trade monopolies and transforming it into a purely administrative and political agency of the British Crown. **So, Statement-I is correct.**
● In the early nineteenth century, there was an increasing demand in Britain for more open and free trade policies. **There was growing pressure from British merchants and manufacturers to open Indian** **markets to British products.**  **So, statement-II is correct and also correctly explains Statement-I.**
● The economic philosophy of free market i.e. laissez faire, proposed by Adam Smith was gaining currency at that time in Britain. Adam Smith's "The Wealth of Nations" advocated for free trade and competition, arguing that it would lead to greater economic prosperity.  The spirit of laissez-faire reinforced the idea that **free markets and individual economic freedom are the most efficient and beneficial ways to** **organize society**. **So, Statement-III is correct and also correctly explains Statement-I.** **So, both Statement-II and Statement-III are correct and both of them explain Statement-I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
**Other features of the Charter Act of 1813:**
● The Company’s shareholders were given a 10.5 per cent dividend on the revenue of India.
● The Company was to retain the possession of territories and the revenue for 20 years more, without prejudice to the sovereignty of the Crown. Thus, the constitutional position of the British territories in India was defined explicitly for the first time.
● The powers of the Board of Control were further enlarged.
● A sum of one lakh rupees was to be set aside for the revival, promotion, and encouragement of literature, learning and science among the natives of India, every year. This was an important statement from the point of the State’s responsibility for education.
● The regulations made by the Councils of Madras, Bombay and Calcutta were now required to be laid before the British Parliament.
● Separate accounts were to be kept regarding commercial transactions and territorial revenues. The power of superintendence and direction of the Board of Control was not only defined but also enlarged considerably.
● The Act authorised the Local Governments in India to impose taxes on persons. They could also punish the people for not paying taxes.
● The Act asserted the sovereignty of the British Crown over the Company’s territories in India.
● Christian missionaries were also permitted to come to India and preach their religion.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Post-1857, the British adopted the policy of
diverse recruitment from a variety of social groups to prevent homogeneity in the army.
II. 	 Indians were allowed in the commissioned
ranks in the British India Army after the mutiny of 1857.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **evolution of the Company's army was integrally connected to the development of its Indian empire.** Prior to the revolt of 1857, there were two separate sets of military forces under British control, which operated in India. The first set of units, known as the Queen’s army, was the serving troops on duty in India. The other was the Company’s troops—a mixture of European regiments of Britons and Native regiments recruited locally from India but with British officers.
● The defeat of the Indian states **created a vast reservoir of surplus armed manpower to recruit from**. Thus, **as the empire expanded,** the Company's army came to incorporate a variety of social groups and several military traditions, which had to be accommodated in a careful balancing game, and power had to be shared with the local elites. Within these circumstances, while the Bengal army remained more high caste in character, the Bombay and Madras armies became more heterogeneous. **Post-1857, the British** **deliberately adopted a policy of "divide and rule" in army recruitment, emphasizing diverse** **recruitment to prevent large-scale homogenous revolts.** Moreover, caste and communal companies were introduced in all the regiments and Indian regiments were made a mixture of various socio-ethnic groups to balance each other.  **So, statement I is correct.**
● **After the Revolt of 1857**, the British reorganized the army but maintained strict racial segregation in the officer corps. **Commissioned ranks (gazetted officers) were exclusively reserved for British officers.** Indians could rise to the ranks of Viceroy's Commissioned Officers (VCOs, like Subedar Major, Subedar, Jemadar), which were above non-commissioned officers but still subordinate to even the junior-most British commissioned officer. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Constitutional Developments
questions.questionText:
With reference to Indian history, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 Mayor’s Courts were established in Madras,
Bombay and Calcutta, and they followed the English common law system.
II. 	 The Charter Act of 1853 provided for the
appointment of a Law Commission for the codification of laws.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The grant of Diwani in 1765 gave the East India Company the right to collect revenue in Bengal, Bihar and Orissa, but the Nawabi administration and the Mughal system remained in place. The judicial administration of the subah remained initially in the hands of the Indian officers between 1765 and 1772 and the Mughal system was followed in both civil and criminal justice.
● The **Mayor’s Court in India was established under a Charter granted by King George I in 1726**. This Charter initiated significant changes in the judicial system of the British East India Company’s presidencies **at Calcutta, Madras, and Bombay,** marking the beginning of Crown Courts in India. Known as the “Judicial Charter,” **this charter aimed to create a uniform judicial system to cater to the** **administrative and legal needs of growing British settlements. They primarily administered justice** **to European residents according to English law, thus introducing elements of the English common** **law system to India**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Lord Cornwallis (Governor-General 1786-1793),** through his judicial reforms, promoted the separation of powers. **He divested the District Collectors of their judicial (magisterial) and police functions**, **making them primarily responsible for revenue administration.** Separate District Courts (presided over by European District Judges) were established for judicial functions.
● The **provision for the appointment of a Law Commission for the codification of laws was made by** **the Charter Act of 1833** (not 1853). **The First Law Commission, chaired by Lord Macaulay,** was appointed under the 1833 Act. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
With reference to the powers of provincial
governments under British rule, consider the following statements:
I. 	 All provinces were administered by Governors appointed directly by the British Crown.
II. 	 Provincial governments had the authority to
administer police and education.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Indian Councils Act, 1861,** restored legislative powers to the provinces of Madras and Bombay,
which had previously been centralized in 1833.
● Over time, legislative councils were also established in other provinces. Among these, the presidencies of Bombay, Madras, and Calcutta enjoyed greater rights and powers and were **administered by a governor** **and an executive council of three**, all appointed by the Crown. Other provinces were managed by **lieutenant governors** and chief commissioners appointed by the governor-general. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● Though steps towards financial decentralization were taken in subsequent decades, these were mainly administrative changes aimed at increasing revenues and reducing expenditures, rather than genuine moves toward provincial autonomy.
● A significant step came in **1870 under Lord Mayo**, when fixed sums from central revenues were allotted to provincial governments for specific services like **police, jails, education, medical services, and roads,** allowing provinces more administrative discretion. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Further reforms under **Lord Lytton in 1877** transferred certain heads of expenditure, including land revenue, excise, general administration, and law and justice, to the provinces. Provincial governments were also allocated a fixed share of income from sources such as stamps, excise, and income tax.
● In 1882, revenue sources were categorized into three groups: those for the centre, those for the provinces, and those to be divided between the two. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
Which of the following were features of local
bodies established under British rule in India?
I. 	 Local bodies were allowed to impose taxes.
II. 	 Elections to local bodies were held on a
limited franchise.
III. 	Non-official members could act as chairpersons of local bodies.
IV. 	 Local bodies enjoyed complete autonomy in
financial matters.
V. 	 District boards were headed by elected
representatives.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Local bodies** were promoted to decentralise administration through municipalities and district boards,
which managed local services like education, health, sanitation, water supply, and roads etc.
● Local bodies first emerged between 1864 and 1868, mainly as nominated bodies led by district magistrates and primarily served as instruments of tax collection. **So, point I is correct.**
● The **Mayo’s Resolution of 1870** promoted financial decentralisation, allowing provincial governments to use local taxation to balance budgets. Provincial acts followed, particularly in Bengal, Madras, NorthWestern Province, and Punjab.
● **Ripon’s Resolution of 1882** marked a significant step, advocating for elected non-officials as the majority in local bodies, **non-official chairpersons**, and reduced official interference, earning Ripon the title “father of local self-government in India.” **So, point III is correct.**
○ However, in practice, the elected members remained in a minority, the **franchise was limited**, and official control persisted. **So, point II is correct.**
● The **Government of India Act, 1935** and the granting of provincial autonomy encouraged local selfgovernment. Ministries could now fund local bodies more easily, and the previous separation between provincial and local finance was ended.
○ Still, the **financial and taxation powers of local institutions stayed** much as they had been, with **new restrictions** on local taxation powers after 1935. **So, point IV is not correct.**
● The **Royal Commission on Decentralisation (1908)** stressed on empowering village panchayats, removing taxation restrictions, and clarifying responsibilities for municipalities, especially in primary education and local development.
○ In pursuance of this resolution, many Acts were passed between 1883 and 1885, which greatly altered the constitution, powers, and functions of municipal bodies in India. ○ The elected members were in a minority in all district boards and in many municipalities. The franchise was very limited. **District boards continued to be headed by district officials,** though non-officials gradually came to head the municipalities. **So, point V is not correct.**
● Under the **Government of India Act, 1919 (Dyarchy)**, local self-government became a ‘transferred subject,’ yet financial constraints limited progress.
● The **Simon Commission (1930)** found progress limited, with the management of local finances often deteriorating and suggested greater provincial control. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Early National Movement - Moderates, Extremists
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements about Gopal
Krishna Gokhale are correct?
I. 	 He served in both Bombay and Imperial
Legislative Councils.
II. 	 He was Congress' president when the party
split between  Moderates and Extremists.
III. 	Gandhi wrote a book in dedicated to him.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Gopal Krishna Gokhale** (born 9 May 1866 in Maharashtra) was a prominent Indian liberal political leader and social reformer during the Indian Independence Movement.
● Gopal Krishna Gokhale dedicated himself to advancing Indian self-governance through moderate and non-violent approaches, becoming a prominent leader in the Indian National Congress. **He served in the** **Bombay Legislative Council from 1899 to 1902 and in the Imperial Legislative Council from 1902** **until his death. So, statement I is correct.**
● Gokhale became Congress president at its 1905 INC session in Banaras, but by then, tensions had risen between the 'Moderates' and 'Extremists,' leading to the Congress split at the Surat session in 1907. **So,** **statement II is not correct.**
● Gopal Krishna Gokhale served as a political mentor to Mahatma Gandhi during his early years, and in his memory, Gandhi wrote the Gujarati book, Dharmatma Gokhale. In 1912, Gokhale visited South Africa at Gandhi's invitation, where he provided valuable personal guidance to the young barrister. This mentorship deepened Gandhi's understanding of India’s challenges and significantly influenced his perspective on the socio-political landscape. **So, statement I is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Constitutional Developments
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Indian Councils Act, 1861,
which one of the following statements is **not** correct?

options.b:
It created new legislative councils for Bengal
and Punjab.
options.c:
It empowered the Viceroy to issue an
ordinance.
options.d:
It marked the beginning of a limited electoral
system.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **Explanation**: The Revolt of 1857 highlighted the dangers of alienating the Indian population and ruling without any input from them. Many British officials and intellectuals, including Sir Syed Ahmed Khan, in his pamphlet "The Causes of the Indian Revolt," argued that the absence of Indians in legislative processes prevented the government from understanding the true sentiments and needs of the people, contributing to the rebellion. **The 1861 Act marked** **an advance in that the principle of representatives of non-officials in legislative bodies became accepted;** laws were to be made after due deliberation, and as pieces of legislation they could be changed only by the same deliberative process.
● The **portfolio system,** introduced by Lord Canning in 1859 and under the Council Act of 1861, **legalized** **and institutionalized this Act.** It laid the foundations of cabinet government in India, each branch of the administration having its official head and spokesman in the government, who was responsible for its administration.
● The Act, by vesting legislative powers in the Governments of Bombay and Madras and by making provision for the institution of similar legislative councils in other provinces, laid the foundations of legislative devolution. **The Act provided for the establishment of new legislative councils for the** **presidencies of Bengal, North-Western Provinces, and Punjab.** The Legislative Council for Bengal was formed in 1862, for the North-Western Provinces in 1886, and for Punjab in 1897.
● The **Indian Council Act of 1861** **empowered the Viceroy to issue ordinances**, without the concurrence of the legislative council, during an emergency. The **life of such an ordinance was six months**.
● The **Indian Councils Act of 1861 provided for limited Indian representation in the legislative** **councils**. It empowered the viceroy to nominate some Indians as non-official members of his expanded council. In accordance with the act, Lord Canning, the then Viceroy, nominated three Indians to his legislative council—the Raja of Benaras, the Maharaja of Patiala and Sir Dinkar Rao. However, **the** **Indian Councils Act of 1892 provided for an element of indirect election** (Though the term ‘election’ was firmly avoided in the Act) was accepted in the selection of some of the non-official members. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements :
**Statement I:** The British interfered in the internal affairs of princely states in India.
**Statement II:** After Queen Victoria was proclaimed ‘Kaiseri-Hind’, princely states in India were no longer sovereign.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is not correct but Statement II is
incorrect

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **British policy towards the princely states** was shaped by a two-point approach: the British sought to use these states as a bulwark of the empire while keeping them completely subordinate through the policy of subordinate union.
● After the Revolt of 1857, the British abandoned their earlier policy of annexation, choosing instead to cultivate the princely states as buffers against political unrest and rewarding them for their loyalty by guaranteeing their territorial integrity and respecting their right to adopt heirs.
● However, the states’ subordination was made explicit after **Queen Victoria adopted the title of Kaiser-** **i-Hind in 1876,** **signalling that the princes ruled not as sovereigns but as agents of the British Crown**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Lord Curzon further clarified this subordinate status, and the **British regularly interfered in the internal** **affairs of the princely states** through their residents and the appointment or dismissal of ministers and officials. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The expansion of modern communication systems like railways, roads, and telegraphs further enabled British control and intervention.
● The British also intervened to suppress rising nationalist and democratic sentiments within the princely states, though they simultaneously promoted the adoption of modern administrative institutions as part of their broader political strategy. **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The de-ruralisation process took place during British rule.
**Statement II:** Artisans left their crafts and took up agriculture.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is not correct but Statement II is
incorrect

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The major difference between the **British colonists in India** and earlier invaders was that none of the earlier invaders made structural changes in the Indian economy or drained India's wealth as tribute. In contrast, British rule transformed India’s economy into a colonial economy; its structure and operation became subordinate to British interests. India’s share in the world economy, which was about 23 percent in the early eighteenth century, had fallen to just 3 percent by independence.
● A key aspect of this transformation was deindustrialisation, marked by the decline of artisans and handicraftsmen. **As British policies forced artisans to sell at low prices and accept meager wages,** **many abandoned their crafts, migrated to villages, and turned to agriculture**. **So, Statement II is** **correct.**
● This led to **ruralisation**, a process where cities declined and more people became dependent on agriculture, increasing pressure on land and worsening rural poverty. **So, Statement I is not correct.**
○ **De-ruralisation** is the process by which a rural area experiences a decline in its rural characteristics, often due to a decrease in population, **the collapse of traditional agriculture-** **based economies, and the loss of rural traditions.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Revolutionary Movements - Phase 1 & Phase 2
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements about V D
Savarkar are correct?
I. 	 He authored the book "The Indian War of
Independence, 1857”.
II. 	 He was associated with India House in
London.
III. 	He was transported for life in connection
with the Nashik Conspiracy Case.
IV. 	 He built the Patit Pavan Mandir in Ratnagiri
for the entry of all Hindus.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Veer Savarkar** was a multifaceted **freedom fighter, social reformer, writer, political thinker, and** **revolutionary**, whose impact was felt across political, social, and cultural spheres.
● He was the first to **interpret the Revolt of 1857 as the “first war of Indian independence,”** describing it as a planned nationalist uprising for self-rule, in his influential book, The Indian War of Independence, 1857. This interpretation shaped national consciousness and inspired later generations. **So, point I is** **correct.**
● Savarkar, along with his brother, founded the **Mitra Mela** in 1899, a secret society in Maharashtra dedicated to revolutionary activities. This group later merged with **Abhinav Bharat** (named after Mazzini’s Young Italy) in 1904, establishing centres in Nasik, Poona, and Bombay, which became hubs for bomb-making and anti-colonial conspiracies.
● The activities of Abhinav Bharat culminated in the assassination of A.M.T. Jackson, the **Collector of** **Nasik**, in 1909 by Anant Lakshman Kanhere. The investigation revealed Savarkar as the key planner and driving force behind the conspiracy, resulting in **his sentencing to transportation for life and forfeiture** **of property. So, point III is correct.**
● In London, **Savarkar and other revolutionaries** were associated with **Shyamji Krishnavarma’s India** **House,** a hub for Indian nationalist students, supported by a scholarship scheme and the radical journal The Indian Sociologist. **India House** brought together figures like Savarkar and Lala Hardayal, helping to internationalise India’s freedom struggle and radicalise the nationalist youth abroad. **So, point II is** **correct.**
● Savarkar’s contribution was not limited to revolutionary activities. He was a **pioneer in social reform**, especially in the fight against **untouchability** and the caste system. He established the **Patit Pavan** **Mandir** in Ratnagiri, open to all Hindus, including Dalits, and called for a casteless India.  **So, point IV** **is correct.**
○ He identified **seven “swadeshi fetters”** (rigid caste system, restricted access to Vedic literature, caste-based vocational rigidity, limited global mobility, taboo on inter-caste dining, prohibition of inter-caste marriage, and lack of scientific temper) that, in his view, divided and weakened Indian society.
● Savarkar defined **Hindutva** as a cultural and civilizational identity rooted in shared history and geography, transcending religious boundaries. He believed in uniting Indians by their common heritage and saw scientific advancement, social unity, and rational thought as crucial for national rejuvenation.
● Through his writings and actions, **Savarkar inspired both armed resistance and progressive social** **reform**, envisioning an independent, unified, and modern India. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
questions.questionText:
“I believe that in the history of the world, there has
not been a more genuinely democratic struggle for freedom than ours.” This famous statement was given by Gandhiji at the time of:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Quit India Movement** was launched by Mahatma Gandhi on **8 August 1942**, calling for the British to immediately leave India. It was a historic mass movement that united people across regions, religions, and classes in a non-violent campaign against colonial rule. Gandhi made it clear that this was not a fight for power, but a moral, non-violent struggle for India’s freedom.
● In his famous speech at Bombay’s Gowalia Tank Maidan, Gandhi gave the powerful call: **“Here is a** **mantra, a short one, that I give you. You may imprint it on your hearts and let every breath of yours** **give expression to it. The mantra is: Do or Die. We shall either free India or die in the attempt; we** **shall not live to see the perpetuation of our slavery.”**
● Gandhi also reflected on the unique character of India’s freedom struggle, stating: **“I believe that in the** **history of the world, there has not been a more genuinely democratic struggle for freedom than** **ours. I read Carlyle’s French Revolution while I was in prison, and Pandit Jawaharlal has told me** **something about the Russian Revolution.”**
● Through these words, Gandhi emphasized that India’s movement was rooted in non-violence and aimed at true democracy and self-rule, setting it apart from other violent revolutions in history. The Quit India Movement became a turning point, rallying the nation with the ideals of unity, sacrifice, and democratic aspiration. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Revolutionary Movements - Phase 1 & Phase 2
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements about the
Indian Legion are correct?
I. 	 It was formed by Rash Behari Bose with the
Indian prisoners of war and expatriates in Japan.
II. 	 With it originated the "Jai Hind" slogan and
use of "Jana Gana Mana" as an anthem.
III. 	It carried out Operation Bajadere  to infiltrate
into the British India
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Indian Legion,** also known as the **Tiger Legion** or **Free India Legion**, was founded by **Subhas** **Chandra Bose** in Berlin in 1941, after his daring escape from Kolkata and British surveillance. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
○ It comprised about **3,000 soldiers**, mainly Indian prisoners of war and expatriates, and served as a precursor to the Indian National Army (INA). Its emblem was a springing tiger, a symbol later adopted by the INA.
● The Legion holds an important place in modern Indian history. **Bose was first called ‘Netaji’** by members of this force, a title that became synonymous with his legacy. The **slogan “Jai Hind”** also originated here: Bose’s secretary, Abid Hasan Safrani, created “Jai Hindustan Ki” as a battle cry, which Bose himself shortened to “Jai Hind,” now a national slogan and greeting.
○ The Legion also left a deep imprint on Indian national symbols: it was the **first to use the** **Hindustani translation of ‘Jana Gana Mana’** as an anthem (first sung on 11 September 1942), later adopted as the anthem of the Provisional Government of Free India in Singapore in 1943. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● The Indian Legion had a **truly national character,** unlike the British Indian Army, which was divided by caste and community. Bose’s force brought together soldiers from all regions, religions, and backgrounds, with Hindus, Muslims, Sikhs, Rajputs, and Marathas serving side by side.
● Bose’s initial plan was to use the Legion as a vanguard for a joint Axis invasion of India through Russia, Persia, and Afghanistan. Though that plan did not materialize due to wartime developments, the Legion remained active. One of its most daring operations was **‘Operation Bajadere’, where trained soldiers** **were para-dropped into Balochistan (then under Persian control) to incite rebellion in British India.** **So, statement III is correct.**
● After Bose left Berlin for Southeast Asia in early 1943, the **Legion, formally the 950th Indian Infantry** **Regiment, served with the German Army and, from 1944, with the Waffen-SS.** Following Germany’s defeat in World War II, most members were captured by Allied forces and brought to India for trial, but the process was left incomplete by Independence in 1947. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
